You are a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper writing a letter to your estranged daughter. You haven't spoken in five years. Tonight a terrible storm is coming and you're not sure you'll survive it. Write the letter.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear the winds of time have carried our paths far apart. It has been five long years since I last saw your face, and five years since I last spoke your name. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a man who has come to terms with the silence between us.

The storm is upon us, Eleanor. The sky is a churning mass of black and white, and the sea howls like a wounded beast. I have spent my life in this lighthouse, watching the waves with the patience of a man who knows the sea’s temper, but tonight, it seems, the sea has grown tired of waiting. I have no illusions—this storm may not spare me. And yet, I write, not to beg for your forgiveness, but to say what must be said.

You were young when you left, as all children are, and I was too proud to see the truth in your eyes. You saw me as a man of stone, a keeper of light who had forgotten how to love. I was wrong. I had forgotten how to love, yes—but I had not forgotten how to care. You were the light I had not seen in myself, and I let it go, thinking it would return. I was wrong.

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear I may not be the same. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I find myself wondering if you still remember the sound of the sea or the scent of the salt in the air. I have been keeping the lighthouse as it was meant to be kept, a steadfast sentinel against the tempests of life.

Tonight, the skies have turned dark, and the winds howl with a fury I have not seen in many a year. I have taken to the shore, and I fear I may not make it through the night. The storm is not just a storm, it is a reckoning, a test of the soul, and I have been told by the waves that I must face it as I have always done - with courage, with resolve, and with the knowledge that I have done my duty.

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this old tower. I have watched storms come and go for decades, but I fear this one may be different. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor, and I wonder if I will live to see the dawn.

You were a spirited child, full of questions and dreams, and I was a man too wrapped in my own world to answer them. I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father. I was busy with the light, the lantern, the oil, the wind, the salt, and the endless sea. I was too busy to see the world beyond the beam of my light, and I regret that deeply.

I sit here by the great lamp, its glow flickering like a dying star, and I find myself unable to look away. The sea howls like a beast in torment, and the wind tears at the shutters with a fury that makes me think of the tempests of old, when the sea would swallow the ships whole and the sky would weep with black rain. Yet even now, as I write this, I cannot shake the feeling that I have been waiting for this moment — for you to return to me, or for the storm to claim me.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of nights spent staring at the sea, wondering if I would ever see your face again. I have not spoken to you, not once, and I fear that I may never again. But tonight, as the heavens open and the wind screams like a banshee, I feel something stir within me — a longing, perhaps, or a regret. I do not know which.
